units.title: Going shopping
units.theme: Shopping Habits: Online vs In Store

questions: Do you prefer online shopping or shopping in stores? Why?
What was the last thing you bought?
What did Aziza buy online?
What advantage of online shopping does Murat mention?
What advantage of instore shopping does Aziza mention?

discussionPrompts: Group discussion: “Online shopping will replace all physical stores in the next 10 years.” Do you agree or disagree? Why?
Role‑play: Work in pairs. One of you is an online store owner, the other is a traditional store owner. Each of you should try to convince the other that your type of store is better.

units.title: Best friends forever!
units.theme: What Makes a True Friend?

questions: What do you think makes a good friend?
Who is your best friend? How long have you known them?
According to Dilnoza, what is the most important thing in friendship?
What example does Dilnoza give about her best friend?
What qualities of a true friend do Dilnoza and Timur mention? (list at least)

discussionPrompts: Do you agree with Dilnoza and Timur? What qualities are most important to you?
Think about your best friend. How do they show these qualities?
In pairs, discuss: “Can you be friends with someone who is not loyal? Why or why not?”

units.title: Fun and games
units.theme: My Favorite Sport and Why I Love It

questions: What role do sports play in your life? Are they important to you?
Do you prefer team sports or individual sports? Explain your preference.
Who is a sportsperson you admire? What do you admire about them?
What specific event made Aziza fall in love with football?
According to Aziza, what are the three main reasons she loves football?
Why does Aziza admire Cristiano Ronaldo specifically? What qualities does she highlight?
What advice does Aziza give to someone who wants to start playing football?

discussionPrompts: Personal Response: Aziza says football is “an integral part of who I am.” Is there an activity, hobby, or sport that you feel the same way about? Describe it and explain its importance in your life.

Discussion: “Hard work and determination are more important than natural talent.” Do you agree or disagree with this statement? Use Aziza’s example of Ronaldo and your own ideas to support your opinion.

Role Play: Imagine you are interviewing a famous athlete (like Ronaldo or Messi). Prepare 5–8 questions you would ask them about their career, motivation, and advice for young people. Then, in pairs, act out the interview.

units.title: Extreme weather
units.theme: Surviving a Natural Disaster: Real Stories

questions: Have you ever experienced any extreme weather? What happened?
What do you think is the most important thing to do during a natural disaster?
Can you name any natural disasters that happen in your country?
Where did each story take place? Story 1: __________ Story 2: __________ Story 3: __________
How did the Miller family survive the flood?
What did Carlos learn from his experience during Hurricane Maria?
How did Bat‑Erdene survive the blizzard in Mongolia?

discussionPrompts: Yulduz mentioned three lessons from these stories. What are they?
Which story affected you the most? Why?
Group discussion: “If a natural disaster happened in your city, what would you do? How would you prepare?”

units.title: You made it!
units.theme: The Best Day of My Life

questions: Describe a time when you felt truly proud of yourself. What had you achieved?
What role does family support play in achieving personal goals?
Do you think it’s more important to win or to do your best? Explain your opinion.
What specific preparations did Gulnaz make for the competition, and how did she feel the day before?
How did her mother’s presence and actions influence Gulnaz’s performance on stage?
Gulnaz mentions her mind “went blank” initially. What helped her overcome this fear?
What did Gulnaz realize was more important than winning the competition itself?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Gulnaz says the day taught her that “if you work hard, believe in yourself, and have just one person who believes in you too, you can achieve things you never thought possible.” Do you agree with this statement? Why or why not? Give an example from your own life or someone you know.
Group Discussion: “Conquering your own fears is a greater achievement than any award.” Discuss this idea in your group.

Creative Writing / Speaking: Describe your own “best day” in detail. Focus not just on what happened, but also on your feelings, the atmosphere, the people involved, and why it remains unforgettable. Use some of the new vocabulary from the podcast.

units.title: Take care of yourself
units.theme: Healthy Habits That Changed My Life

questions: In your opinion, what is the biggest challenge teenagers face today regarding their health?
Do you think it's easier to maintain physical health or mental health? Why?
What does the phrase "start small" mean to you when it comes to making life changes?
According to Doctor Karimova, what crucial role does sleep play in learning and memory?
What specific advice does she give for improving "sleep hygiene"?
Beyond structured sports, what other examples of physical activity does she suggest?
What is Doctor Karimova's philosophy on eating fast food?
Why does she emphasize the importance of breakfast, and what healthy options does she suggest?
What is Doctor Karimova's main advice for managing stress and maintaining mental health?
What is her final, overarching tip for building healthier habits?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Doctor Karimova says, "asking for help is a sign of strength, not weakness." Do you agree with this statement? Why can it be difficult for teenagers to ask for help, especially with mental health?

Group Discussion: "Small, consistent changes are far more sustainable than one big overhaul." Discuss this idea. Share examples of a habit you successfully changed (or tried to change) and whether a smallsteps approach worked better.

Action Plan: Based on the advice in the podcast, create your own personal "Healthy Habit Action Plan". Choose one habit you want to improve (sleep, exercise, nutrition, or mental health). Write down: Your specific, small goal (e.g., "I will go to bed 20 minutes earlier.") The steps you will take to achieve it. How you will track your progress. One potential obstacle and how you will overcome it.

units.title: Sound check
units.theme: My Music Taste: From Classical to Pop

questions: How much does your musical taste define your personality? Can you tell a lot about a person from the music they listen to?
Is there a particular song or piece of music that is closely linked to a strong memory or emotion for you? Describe it.
Do you think the music popular today has the same depth and quality as music from previous decades? Why or why not?
According to Umida, what makes pop music appealing and accessible?
Alisher appreciates both rock and classical music. What specific qualities does he attribute to each genre?
How do both Umida and Alisher use music to manage or reflect their emotions?
What role does traditional music play in their lives, according to the conversation?

discussionPrompts: Comparative Analysis: Compare and contrast Umida's and Alisher's musical tastes. What do their preferences reveal about their personalities, based on what they said? Do you agree with their assessments of the genres?

Personal Reflection: Umida calls music "a friend who understands your every mood." Do you share this sentiment? Describe how you use music as a tool for emotional regulation or personal expression. Be specific about genres or artists.

Group Discussion: Alisher values "structural complexity and lyrical depth" in music, while Umida enjoys the "pure, unadulterated fun" of pop. Can music that is simple and fun be just as artistically valuable as complex music? Debate this within your group, using examples from your own musical knowledge.

units.title: Amazing architecture
units.theme: My Dream House: What Would It Look Like?

questions: Where do you currently live? What do you like or dislike about it?
If you could design your perfect home, what three features would be most important to you? Why?
Do you prefer living in a busy city or a quiet countryside? Explain your preference.
Where would Umida's dream house be located, and why does she choose this location?
What materials would be used for the exterior of the house, and why?
List three special features Umida mentions for her dream house.
According to Umida, what should a home truly represent or feel like?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Umida says a home should be "a place where you feel completely at peace." What does "peace" mean to you in the context of a living space? Describe the atmosphere of your ideal home.

Speaking Practice: Describe your own dream house in detail. Include information about its location, size, design, special features, and why it would be perfect for you. Try to speak for at least 2 minutes.
Pair Discussion: Discuss with a partner: Is it better to have a large, impressive house or a small, cozy one? What are the advantages and disadvantages of each?

units.title: The future is now!
units.theme: Life in 2050: Predictions and Dreams

questions: When you imagine the year 2050, what's the first thing that comes to your mind?
Do you think technology will solve most of our problems or create new ones?
According to Timur, what are two ways technology might change our daily lives by 2050?
What is Aysanem's main concern about advanced technology like brain chips?
What environmental solutions does Timur hope to see by 2050?
What does Timur say is the key to true happiness?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: What makes you truly happy – technology, relationships, or something else?
Group Discussion: "Technology should serve us, not control us." Discuss what this means.
Speaking Practice: Imagine you are living in 2050. Describe a typical day in your life. Speak for at least 2 minutes.

units.title: Animals in danger
units.theme: Should We Protect Endangered Animals?

questions: What comes to mind when you hear the phrase "endangered animals"? Can you name any?
In your opinion, do humans have a responsibility to protect other species? Why or why not?
If you had to choose between spending a large sum of money on a new hospital or on saving a critically endangered animal, which would you choose and why?
According to Bayram, what are the three main reasons we should protect endangered animals?
What is Ozoda's primary counterargument regarding the urgency of human needs?
How does Bayram argue that protecting animals and helping humans are "interconnected"? What examples does he give?
Ozoda presents a powerful counterargument about a village in Africa. Summarize this argument in your own words.
What "integrated solution" does Bayram suggest at the end to address both human and animal needs?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: After listening to both sides of the debate, where do you stand? Has your opinion changed or become stronger? Explain your position, referring to specific arguments from the podcast.

Group Discussion: "The needs of the many outweigh the needs of the few." Does this principle apply to the debate about saving endangered species versus helping human populations? Discuss the ethical complexities.

RolePlay / Debate: In small groups, conduct your own debate on this topic. One person should act as the moderator, and two others should present the opposing views. Use arguments from the podcast and add your own ideas. The rest of the group can act as the audience and ask questions at the end.

units.title: Off to school
units.theme: School Life Around the World

questions: What do you think school is like in other countries?
If you could ask a student from another country about their school, what would you ask?
What time does school start and finish in Japan?
What do Brazilian students do during break time?
What interesting habit do Egyptian teachers have?
What similarities do the three schools share?
According to all the students, what is the best thing about school?

discussionPrompts: Talk about your own school. How is it different from the schools mentioned in this podcast?
What is your favorite thing about your school? Why?
If you were to write a comment for this podcast, what would you say?

units.title: Getting around
units.theme: Travel Tips: How to Plan a Perfect Trip

questions: Have you ever traveled to another city or country? What was your experience like?
What do you think is the most important thing to prepare before a trip?
Do you prefer to plan every detail of a trip or be spontaneous? Why?
Why does Sabir recommend making a checklist before traveling?
According to Aygul, what is the biggest mistake people make when packing?
What should you always pack in a small carry‑on bag, and why?
What does Sabir suggest doing to stay safe with money and cards?
What happened on Sabir’s trip to Istanbul? How did it turn into a positive experience?

discussionPrompts: Personal reflection: Which of the four tips do you think is the most useful? Why?
Speaking practice: Describe a trip you took (real or imaginary). Explain how you prepared, what you packed, and any unexpected moments you experienced.
Group discussion: Sabir says, “Some of the best travel experiences come from unplanned detours.” Do you agree or disagree? Share examples from your own life or people you know.
Writing task: Write a short guide (150–200 words) for first‑time travelers. Include three key tips based on the podcast and add one of your own.

units.title: nofilter
units.theme: Social Media: Good or Bad?

questions: Which social media platforms do you use? Which one do you spend the most time on?
How much time do you spend on social media each day? Do you think it is a lot or a little?
Do social media bring more benefit or more harm to your life? Explain your opinion.
What three advantages of social media did Ilhom mention?
What three problems of social media did Sarbinaz list?
What advice did Ilhom and Sarbinaz give for using social media safely? Write at least four pieces of advice.
What did they mean by saying "social media is like a knife"?
In their opinion, how much time is healthy to spend on social media?

discussionPrompts: Personal opinion: Are you on Ilhom’s side or Sarbinaz’s side? Why? Give an example from your own experience.
Group discussion: Do you agree with the statement “Social media increases loneliness, it does not reduce it”? Why or why not? Discuss in your group.

Practical task: For one week, keep track of how much time you spend on social media. Write down the time each day. At the end of the week, analyze: did you notice you spent a lot of time? Do you want to make any changes?
Writing task: Write your thoughts on “How to use social media in a healthy way”. What rules should you follow? What would you like to change about your own habits? (100–150 words)

units.title: Let's cook!
units.theme: My Grandmother's Best Recipe

questions: What role does food play in your family's gatherings? Is there a dish that is always present?
Do you think family recipes should be kept secret or shared with everyone? Why?
Describe a moment in your life when a taste or smell brought back a strong memory.
What does Aziza say is the most important ingredient in her grandmother's cooking?
List three specific details that make her grandmother's plov unique (ingredients or preparation methods).
What did Aziza learn from cooking with her grandmother, beyond the recipe itself?
According to Aziza, why should people ask their elders about family recipes?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Think of a dish that has special meaning in your family. Who makes it best? What memories are attached to it? Describe it in detail.

Speaking Practice: Prepare a 2‑minute talk about a traditional dish from your culture. Explain its ingredients, how it is prepared, and what it represents for your family or community. Use at least five new words from the vocabulary list.
Writing Task: Write a short story about a time you cooked (or ate) something memorable. Describe the setting, the smells, the tastes, and the people who were with you. Aim for 150‑200 words.

units.title: Modern Lifestyles
units.theme: Working from Home vs Working in an Office

questions: Do you think you would prefer working from home or in an office? Why?
What are the main challenges of working from home?
How important is social interaction at work for you?
What are two advantages of working from home that Murat mentions?
According to Aziza, what are two benefits of working in an office?
How does Murat say he separates work from personal life when working from home?
What solution do both speakers agree on at the end? Why do they think it's the best?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Which side do you lean towards – working from home or in an office? Explain your reasoning based on your personality and lifestyle.
Group Discussion: Do you think companies should give employees the freedom to choose their work environment? What challenges might that create?
Roleplay: In pairs, act out a conversation between an employee who wants to work from home and a manager who prefers employees to be in the office. Use arguments from the podcast and add your own.
Writing Task: Write a short essay (150200 words) on the topic: "The future of work: will most people work from home or in offices in 10 years?" Give reasons for your opinion.

units.title: Lights, camera, action!
units.theme: My All‑Time Favorite Movie

questions: What is a film that has had a strong emotional impact on you? What made it so powerful?
Do you prefer films based on true stories or purely fictional ones? Why?
What does the phrase “never give up” mean to you personally?
What is the film “The Pursuit of Happyness” based on?
What specific scene does Gulnara describe as “heartbreaking”? What happens in that scene?
What were three reasons Gulnara gives for loving this film?
What does Gulnara say the film reminds her of in her own life?
What message does Gulnara want listeners to take away from the film?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Gulnara connects the film to her father. Is there a film that reminds you of someone in your life? Describe that connection.

Speaking Practice: Prepare a 2‑minute monologue about your favourite film. Include: the title, main actors, a brief summary of the plot, why it matters to you, and one memorable scene. Use at least five new vocabulary words from the list.
Group Discussion: “Films based on true stories are always more powerful than fictional ones.” Do you agree or disagree? Discuss with examples.
Writing Task: Write a short review of a film you recently watched. Explain the story, your opinion, and whether you would recommend it. (150‑200 words)

units.title: Getting the message
units.theme: How to Express Your Opinion Without Fighting

questions: Think of a recent disagreement you had. How did it end – well or badly? What went wrong?
Do you find it easier to express your opinion in writing or face to face? Why?
What does the phrase “agree to disagree” mean to you? Is it easy or hard to accept?
According to Rayxan and Ulugbek, what is the main reason people argue about opinions?
What example do they use to practice the tips? What compromise do they reach?
What does Ulugbek say is the real goal of a disagreement?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Which of the four tips do you think you need to practice most? Why?
Group Discussion: “Agree to disagree is just an excuse to avoid difficult conversations.” Do you agree or disagree? Discuss.

Role‑play: In pairs, choose one of the topics below. One person takes one side, the other takes the opposite side. Practice having a calm discussion using the tips from the podcast. Should students wear school uniforms? Is homework necessary? Should exams be replaced with continuous assessment?
Writing Task: Write a short reflection (150‑200 words) on a time you handled a disagreement well – or wish you had handled it better. What did you learn?

units.title: We love celebs!
units.theme: If I Were Famous...

questions: What comes to your mind when you think of the word “fame”? Is it something you desire or something you fear?
If you could be famous for one thing, what would you want it to be? Why?
Do you think famous people have easier or harder lives than ordinary people? Give reasons.
What kind of fame does Aziza say she would not want, and why?
List three positive things Aziza mentions about being famous.
List three negative aspects of fame that Aziza discusses.
How does Aziza say she would try to “stay grounded” if she became famous?
What final question does Aziza leave for listeners to think about?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Aziza says, “Fame is a tool, not an identity.” What do you think she means by this? Do you agree? Explain with your own words.
Speaking Practice: Prepare a 2‑minute monologue answering the question: “Would you want to be famous? Why or why not?” Use at least five new vocabulary words from the list.
Group Discussion: Is it fair that society demands perfection from celebrities while ordinary people are allowed to make mistakes? Discuss with examples.
Writing Task: Imagine you are a famous person for a day. Write a short diary entry (150‑200 words) describing what you did, how you felt, and whether you enjoyed the experience.

units.title: The world of work
units.theme: The world of work

questions: What career ideas do you have for yourself right now? How did you become interested in them?
Do you think it's better to specialize early or keep your options open? Why?
What does the phrase “work‑life balance” mean to you? Do you think it's important?
What three‑step framework does Azizbek suggest for choosing a career?
According to Azizbek, why is having many interests a “strength, not a weakness”?
What is the difference between university education and vocational training? Give examples from the podcast.
Why does Azizbek say soft skills are “essential and often underestimated”? Name at least three soft skills mentioned.
What are the four pieces of advice Azizbek gives to students who want to start building their career?
How does Azizbek describe the ideal career?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Azizbek says, “Failure is not the opposite of success; it's part of it.” Do you agree? Describe a time you learned something valuable from a failure.

Speaking Practice: In pairs, conduct your own “career counseling” session. One person plays the student, the other the counselor. The student shares their interests and strengths; the counselor offers suggestions on possible careers and steps to take. Use vocabulary from the podcast.
Group Discussion: Should schools do more to help students explore careers? What activities or programs would you suggest?

Writing Task: Write a short essay (150‑200 words) describing your ideal career. Explain why you are drawn to it, what skills and education it requires, and what steps you can take now to work toward it.

units.title: Making plans
units.theme: My Plans for the Next Five Years

questions: When you imagine your life five years from now, what do you see? What are you doing? Where are you living?
Do you prefer making detailed plans or going with the flow? Why?
What does the phrase “the journey is just as important as the destination” mean to you personally?
What two university subjects is Ayjamal considering? What draws her to them?
What specific goals does she set for her second year of university?
What does she hope to achieve by her third year (2028) in terms of language, experience, and skills?
What will her focus be in her final year? What decision will she have to make?
By the time she is 22, what does Ayjamal hope to have in her life? List three things.
What does she say about plans changing? How does she view that possibility?

discussionPrompts: Personal Reflection: Ayjamal says she wants to be “still curious – still learning, still growing, still open.” What does this mean to you? What would you add to your own five‑year vision beyond career and education?

Speaking Practice: Prepare a 2‑3 minute monologue about your own plans for the next five years. Use the structure: first year, second year, etc., or organize by themes (education, career, personal growth, travel). Include at least five new vocabulary words from the list.
Group Discussion: Is it better to have a rigid plan or to stay flexible? What are the advantages and disadvantages of each approach? Share examples from your own experience or people you know.
Writing Task: Write a letter to your future self five years from now. Describe who you hope to be, what you hope to have achieved, and what values you want to hold onto. Aim for 200‑250 words.
